metadata.career_relevance: HDFC Bank processes 50,000 loan application documents daily — salary slips, bank statements, identity proofs. Before AI: 200 agents manually extract data (name, income, employer) from each PDF. After AI: Spring AI extracts all fields in 2 seconds per document, flagged fields go to human review. Zerodha processes 100,000 trade confirmations (contract notes) nightly — AI extracts trade details into structured records. These are not research projects; they're running in production today. After this topic, you can build AI-powered data pipelines that save hundreds of person-hours per week.

hook.real_world_problem:
HDFC Bank loan processing without AI extraction:

```
PROBLEM:
  Salary slip (PDF) → agent reads manually:
  - Name: 'Raj Kumar Sharma'
  - Employer: 'Wipro Technologies Ltd'
  - Monthly Gross: ₹85,000
  - Employment Date: 12 March 2022
  Time per document: 8 minutes
  Volume: 50,000/day
  Staff needed: 667 agents × ₹30,000/month = ₹2 crore/month

WITH AI EXTRACTION:
  PDF text → Spring AI → ExtractedSalarySlip {
    applicantName: 'Raj Kumar Sharma',
    employer: 'Wipro Technologies Ltd',
    monthlyGrossIncome: 85000,
    employmentDate: '2022-03-12',
    extractionConfidence: 94
  }
  Time: 2 seconds per document
  Staff: 50 agents (review only 6% flagged for low confidence)
  Cost reduction: 93% (₹2 crore → ₹1.5 lakh/month)
```
hook.what_you_will_build: AI-powered salary slip extractor and support ticket classifier for HDFC Bank.

visual_aid.description: Three AI feature pipelines side by side. Left (Extraction): PDF text → ChatClient (extract prompt + BeanOutputConverter schema) → SalarySlipExtract record → DB. Center (Classification): Support ticket text → ChatClient (classify prompt) → TicketClassification record → routing (urgency 5 → human, 1-3 → automated). Right (Summarization): Long document (500 words) → ChatClient (summarize in 3 bullets) → SummaryResult → dashboard card. Each shows the same pattern: text in → structured record out.
visual_aid.alt_text: AI feature pipelines: extraction, classification, summarization — all producing structured Java records

worked_example.scenario: HDFC salary slip extractor with confidence scoring and fallback.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. SalarySlipExtract record with all fields
2. SalarySlipExtractor: extract() returns typed record
3. extractWithFallback(): returns Optional.empty() if confidence < 70
4. BatchExtractor: process list of salary slips, separate high/low confidence
5. @SpringBootTest: extractor and classifier wired up

step_by_step_solution.details: Ask the AI to rate its own confidence in the extraction (0-100). This isn't perfect but it's directionally accurate — the AI knows when it's uncertain. Use confidence < 70 to flag documents for human review. This creates a human-AI hybrid: AI handles 94% automatically, humans review the 6% flagged cases. Better than 100% human review (slow) or 100% AI (errors). Rule: always have a fallback path for low-confidence AI output.

worked_example.final_code:
// extractWithFallback: if confidence < 70 → Optional.empty() → route to human agent
// BatchExtractor: split list into auto-processed (high confidence) vs review queue (low)
worked_example.expected_output:
SalarySlipExtract{
  applicantName='Raj Kumar Sharma',
  employer='Wipro Technologies Ltd',
  monthlyGrossIncomePaise=8500000,
  employmentDate='2022-03-12',
  extractionConfidence=94
}
things_to_notice: Amounts in paise (not rupees): eliminates decimal precision issues in Java. ₹85,000 = 8,500,000 paise. Always work with integers for money.
Temperature=0.0 for extraction: you want deterministic, consistent extraction — not creative interpretation. Same document should always extract the same fields.
BeanOutputConverter.getFormat() in the prompt: without this, the AI doesn't know what JSON structure you expect. Include it as a parameter in the user message.
Batch processing: for 50,000 documents, use virtual threads (Java 21) or reactive pipeline. Don't process sequentially — parallelize across N AI calls with rate limit awareness.

industry_examples.use_case: 100,000 trade contract notes (PDFs) arrive nightly from NSE/BSE. Spring AI batch job: extract trade date, symbol, quantity, price, brokerage from each PDF text. Structured records go to reconciliation DB. Previously: 12 agents, 8 hours overnight. Now: AI batch job, 45 minutes, 3 agents review flagged extractions. Trade reconciliation accuracy improved from 97.2% (manual errors) to 99.8%.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How would you add AI document processing to our loan system?' → Structured extraction with Spring AI. BeanOutputConverter: AI returns JSON, Spring converts to typed Java record. Fields: all required document fields + extractionConfidence. Low confidence (< 70) → human review queue. Batch: virtual threads for parallel processing, Bucket4j for API rate limiting. Temperature=0 for consistent extraction. Test with real sample documents before production deployment.
applications.real_world_workflow: AI extraction workflow: (1) extract text from PDF (Apache PDFBox or AWS Textract), (2) build structured extraction prompt with BeanOutputConverter schema, (3) call AI with temperature=0, (4) parse JSON response to Java record, (5) check confidence threshold — route low-confidence to review queue, (6) validate critical fields (PAN format, date ranges), (7) store in DB + audit log for compliance.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using AI extraction output directly without confidence checking. AI returns extractionConfidence=45 (uncertain) but the code uses the data anyway — incorrect income extracted, loan approved incorrectly.
common_gaps.remediation: Always gate on confidence: if extractionConfidence < 70, route to human review queue. Don't pass low-confidence extractions downstream. Include confidence in every extraction record. Alert if average confidence drops below 80% (may indicate document format change).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Code calls extract() and immediately uses the result without checking confidence — no if (result.extractionConfidence() < threshold) branch

key_takeaways: BeanOutputConverter<T>: AI returns JSON → automatically converts to Java record
Temperature=0 for extraction: deterministic, consistent — same doc → same output
Confidence field: AI rates its own certainty — use < 70 to flag for human review
Paise not rupees: always integers for money in AI extraction
Batch processing: virtual threads for parallelism, Bucket4j for rate limiting
Classification: urgency + category + requiresHuman → automated routing
Summarization: max 3 bullet points, one sentence each — constraints in system prompt
Fallback path: ALWAYS have a non-AI path for when AI is wrong or unavailable
Temperature=0 + seed: reproducible output for testing
Audit every AI extraction: store prompt + response + confidence for compliance

self_check_questions.question: HDFC's AI extracts loan income as ₹85,000 from a salary slip. The actual slip says '₹85,000 gross, ₹72,000 net'. The AI returned monthlyGrossIncome=85000 with confidence=88. Should this be auto-approved or sent to human review?
self_check_questions.answer: Confidence=88 > 70 threshold: auto-approve extraction technically. However: this specific case reveals a system design issue. The extraction is correct (gross = 85,000) but: (1) the prompt must explicitly say 'extract GROSS income, not net' — ambiguity in the prompt causes ambiguity in extraction. (2) Downstream validation should check: gross > net (if net is also extracted). (3) Add a 'extractedField' field to clarify which number was used: 'GROSS'. In production: extract BOTH gross and net, store both, use gross for eligibility but flag cases where gross/net ratio > 25% (unusual, may indicate errors). Rule: extraction is only as good as the prompt. If gross vs net is ambiguous in real documents, your prompt must resolve the ambiguity explicitly.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: LangChain4j — an alternative AI framework for Java with AiServices interface magic, built-in memory, and a rich ecosystem of integrations.
